observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer
Conduct a physical inspection of unreleased checks near or on the cut-off date and prepare an accurate list of these checks;
Review the accuracy of the list by cross-verifying the check details with official records, such as the MTO logbook signed by the claimants; and
Ensure the recovery of the service charges erroneously deducted from LBP Account No. 4162-1042-12 by submitting a formal request to the LBP Branch Head. The request should also include a recommendation to address and rectify the issues noted.

We recommended that the accounting personnel who is in-charge of remittance to PhilHealth, under the supervision of the Municipal Accountant, trace back the accounting records, review and settle the variances and deficiencies shown in Tables 1 and 2.  Unremitted withholding from employees and employees share must be remitted immediately to PhilHealth,  while for those remittances made even without equivalent deduction from employees, if any, the corresponding personnel must refund the amount paid from LGU fund.
Lastly, we recommended that the Remittance in Charge ensure prompt and full remittance of all amounts withheld from employees' salaries, including the LGU's corresponding employer share. This should be done in strict compliance with the deadlines set by PhilHealth to prevent penalties and protect employees' entitlement to benefits.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to promptly coordinate with Pag-IBIG to update personnel’s employment status and/or salary adjustments.
Also, we recommended that the personnel in-charge of remittance to Pag-IBIG, under the supervision of the Municipal Accountant, perform the following:
trace back the accounting records, review and settle the variances and deficiencies shown in Table 1;
unremitted withholding from employees and employer’s share, for premium contributions and loan amortization, must be remitted immediately to Pag-IBIG;
give justification for negative balances specifically January and February 2024 presented in table 1; and
henceforth, ensure the full and timely remittance to Pag-IBIG the amounts withheld from employees and the recorded employer’s share, in accordance with the deadline set by Pag-IBIG.
Lastly, we recommended that the Accounting Department should establish and maintain an accurate subsidiary ledger for the "Due to Pag-IBIG" account. This ledger must clearly segregate individual member contributions from loan amortizations to ensure accurate tracking, proper reconciliation, and compliance with reporting requirements.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO), who are also the Municipality’s AAOs:
Regularly reconcile records with GSIS and promptly update employees on any changes to their employment status and salary adjustments.
Ensure timely updates in the GSIS system to maintain accurate records.
Monitor the approval of employees' loan applications and immediately inform payroll preparer to ensure timely deductions from payroll.
In addition, we recommended that the payroll maker ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees' salaries in the month they are due.
Also, the Remittance in Charge should remit the amount withheld with the employer’s contribution to GSIS, within the prescribed deadline.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSIS remittance in charge trace back their records and reconcile the variances listed in Table 1 and review the balance withheld from CY 2023 and prior years that remained unremitted as of December 31, 2024.  Verified unremitted premium contributions and loan amortizations must be remitted to GSIS immediately, while for those remittances made even without equivalent deduction from employees, if any, the corresponding personnel must refund the amount paid from LGU fund.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSIS remittance in-charge conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium contributions and loan amortizations by comparing GSIS billings with actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This practice ensures billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees correct salary deductions.
Lastly, we recommended that the accounting in charge of recording of Due to GSIS account, fast track the reconciliation of records to correct the balances of Subsidiary Ledgers and provide accurate monitoring of remittances of amount withheld from employees and government share.

We recommended that the BAC properly prepare all the necessary documents required in every step of the procurement process, including but not limited to, the scrutiny of required coverage of the performance security posted.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all the necessary documentary requirements for the payment of development projects are complete and submitted before affixing her signature in the Disbursement Voucher and certifying that the supporting documents attached are complied.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit justification for the discrepancies noted in the implementation of Construction/Establishment of the Sanitary Landfill project.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor ensure the compliance of the necessary documents required by COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the acquisition of land and submit the lacking documents enumerated in Paragraph 1.3 to the Audit Team.
The foregoing recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance depending upon management’s compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order for the creation of GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the GFPS and the DILG Operations Field Officer assigned to the LGU, systematically collect and consolidate updated data on gender statistics, including age and sex-disaggregated data. This will facilitate the creation and maintenance of a comprehensive GAD Database to support evidence-based planning, implementation, and monitoring of gender-responsive programs and activities.
Further, we recommended that the GFPS ensure the timely preparation of the Annual Status Report on Institutional Mechanisms for Gender Mainstreaming and submit it to the DILG Regional Office within the prescribed deadline.
Lastly, we recommended that the GAD Focal Person provide a written explanation for the “no allocation / no allocation from LGU” remarks on certain activities, despite the fact that these activities were allocated a budget in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and HRMO develop and issue written policy guidelines outlining the standard procedures for hiring Job Order personnel to ensure objective and impartial selection process, specifying the required documents to be submitted by applicants, the detailed job description in the Contract of Service, the submission of accomplishment reports by JO personnel, and other necessary internal control measures. This will help streamline the hiring process and promote transparency and accountability.
We also recommended that once the policies and/or manual on hiring of job order is prepared, submit a copy to the audit team.

We recommended that the MDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, ensure the proper geotagging of all infrastructure projects funded under the MDRRM Fund, in strict compliance with the provisions of Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) Department Order No. 79, series of 2017, to promote transparency and accurate project monitoring.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the MDRRMO refrain from utilizing the MDRRM Fund to cover expenses for non-disaster related expenditures.
Further, we recommended that all payments of honoraria be accompanied by the required supporting documents, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Finally, the computation of the RP’s honorarium should be presented, in compliance with DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-1, dated April 23, 2007.

We recommended that the assigned personnel coordinate with the Inventory Team, GSO, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Assessor, and Municipal Accountant in identifying the buildings owned by the Municipality on records and in actual count.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take proactive measures to ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002 to mitigate the risk of losses resulting from natural and man-made calamities on properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Local Treasurer, Local Accountant and other Officials concerned, perform the following actions:
Assign separate individuals to perform the roles of Liquidating Officer and Custodian of Accountable Forms in strict compliance with COA Circular No. 92-382, to ensure proper segregation of duties and strengthen internal controls;
Review and revise internal policies or procedures to institutionalize the segregation of duties in cash handling and accountability over forms, minimizing the risk of fraud, errors, or misuse; and
Send the personnel involved in financial transactions to attend related trainings and orientation to reinforce awareness of COA rules and sound internal control practices.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant re-classify the properties held for rent from PPE accounts to Investment Property using the cost model.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the Municipal Engineer to submit to the Audit Team for
review and evaluation all the lacking documents enumerated in
paragraphs 1.8 to 1.12;
b. Henceforth, together with the Inspectorate Committee, conduct
thorough inspection on all government projects during and after
construction to ensure strict conformance with the Program of Works
and Detailed Estimates.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development
Council, prepare the detailed estimates to reflect all the items, quantities and
costs, within plus or minus ten percent (10%), needed to complete the projects.

Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments should be made
based on properly documented obligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Through the department heads concerned, expedite the
implementation of feasible development projects. Otherwise,
reprogram the PPAs to other attainable priority projects.
2. Together with the Sangguniang Bayan, re-appropriate or augment the
savings from implemented PPAs to other development projects needed
by the Municipality.
3. Together with the Municipal Development Council and Sangguniang
Bayan, fund development projects out of the 20% DF that are well-
planned and procurement-and implementation-ready.
4. Require Inspectorate Committee to monitor the implementation of the
projects by the Municipal Engineering Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and MPDO to:
a. submit an explanation on certifying the correctness of the SWA and
Inspection Report despite the noted defects and uninstalled work
items/ materials in the projects and non-deduction thereof from the
progress or final billings of the contracts. This is without prejudice to
the issuance of notice of disallowance for deficiencies, if warranted;
and
b. direct the resident/ project engineers and Inspectorate Team to:
 (i) conduct thorough inspection on all infrastructure projects
 for validation of the actual accomplishment submitted for
 progress billing to make sure that all materials and
 prescribed processes conformed with the approved plan,
 Program of Work and Detailed Estimates or Variation
 Orders, if any; and
 (ii) prepare Inspection Report accurately showing the actual
 work accomplished as compared to the contractor’s SWA
 and make necessary adjustments/ deductions from the
 progress billing equivalent to the cost of noted inaccuracy;
c. require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to ensure
that all contracts of the Municipality incurring delays in completion be
imposed with liquidated damages equivalent to 1/10 of one percent of
the cost of the unfinished portion of the project for each day of delay.

Lastly, we recommended the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) to regularly
monitor all procurement activities of the Municipality and ensure that the
timeline established in Annex C of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 is strictly adhered
to.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Health Officer to submit a written explanation to justify the
noted overpayment of SRA and AHDP granted to Healthcare Workers of
the Municipality of Hilongos;
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that (i) all necessary supporting
documents are attached to the claims before approving all payments and
(ii) correct computation based on the guidelines set by Administrative
Orders and Circulars are effected in determining the amount due to each
claimant to avoid disallowance in audit;
c. Individual recipients, without prejudice to the joint and solidary liability
of those who authorized and certified the legality and propriety of the
payment, refund the overpayment SRA and/ or AHDP.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. issue an Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations for DRRM or his
authorized representative for the receipt of in-kind donations.
b. designate the office to receive all in-kind donations who will:
 • establish an inventory management system on receipt, distribution and/
or utilization of in-kind donations, and
 • prepare the Summary/ List of Donations Received, Distributed and
 Balances every month or every quarter supported by Acknowledgment
 Receipts, Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-
 users, and Inventory of Remaining Balances, and
 • submit the same to the NDRRMC through the OCD, copy furnish the
 Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team within ten (10) working
 days after the end of each month or quarter;
 • submit to the Property Officer copy of the Acknowledgment Receipt for
 donated equipment and other tangible items which costs P15,000 or less
 and has a life of more than a year
c. require the designated Property Custodian to:
 • Maintain Stock Cards/ Property Card for all donations in-kind
 received and update regularly;
 • Maintain Registry of Donated Relief Goods for each kind of inventory
 items;
 • Prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for issuance of
 donated PPEs and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for the issuances of
 tangible items which costs ₱15,000.00 or less and have a life of more
 than a year and are not considered as PPE to establish accountability
 over them and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the PAR and
 ICS; and
d. require the departments responsible for the distribution of relief goods and
other supplies (MDRRMO and DSWDO) to submit Proof of Receipt by and
Distribution to the Beneficiaries/ end-users to the Office referred to in letter (b);
e. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data from
the documents obtained, compare records with Registry of Donated Relief
Goods maintained by the Property Officer or by the designated MDRRMO for
check and balance, and accordingly disclose all necessary information in the
Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) and the Municipal Accountant to:
a. submit to the Audit Team within five (5) days the required lacking
documents enumerated above to prove regularity and legality of the
corresponding disbursements. This is without prejudice to the
issuance of Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, if warranted; and
b. strictly observe the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184
to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness
and public monitoring.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the personnel-in-charge to renew the registration of the five (5)
serviceable vehicles and expedite the registration of four (4) new
vehicles operating without LTO registration.
b. Instruct the Custodian or Driver of the Motor Vehicle to stop the
operation of the vehicle with expired LTO registration.
c. Consider the provision of an adequate garage or shed to house all
motor vehicles, including those for disposal.

We also recommended that the personnel-in-charge and the Property
Custodian:
a. Exhaust all possible means to locate the records of registration not
presented during audit;
b. Keep the records of registration for all LGU vehicles, monitor their
expiry dates and schedule the annual renewal of LTO registration in
coordination with the Property Custodian or Driver of the Motor
Vehicle;
c. Schedule for repair inactive vehicles which can still be repaired within
reasonable cost;
d. Evaluate the unserviceable vehicles for possible disposal; and in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the disposal
procedures unserviceable vehicles as well as those non-existing,
missing, sold and unidentified vehicles, pursuant to pertinent
provisions in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the assigned personnel to:
a. Secure from the GSIS General Insurance Fund (GIF), insurances
covering properties, contracts, rights of action, and other insurable
risks of the LGU;
b. Prepare the Property Inventory Form (PIF) listing of all the insurable
properties and other assets, showing their latest appraised
values/valuation, appraisal date, location, and other information;
c. Extract from the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment, as well as from the Report on the Physical Count of
Inventories, prepared in accordance with the prevailing guidelines, the
data for the PIF pertaining to the insurable assets and interest of the
government (excluding impaired properties for disposal and other
unqualified properties);
d. Gather other necessary data on these properties;
e. Cause the appraisal of the insurable properties and other assets of the
LGU. For this purpose, an in-house appraisal shall be sufficient if the
property or insurable interest has a value of ₱10 million and below.
Otherwise, an independent appraisal shall be necessary;
f. Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team and the GIF, GSIS, not
later than April 30 of each year; and
g. Include in the annual budget the amount of premiums for the general
insurance covering all insurable properties and other assets and ensure
its payment to the GSIS.

Moreover, we recommended that the assigned personnel coordinate with the
Inventory Team, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Assessor and Municipal
Accountant in identifying the buildings owned by the Municipality on records
and in actual count.

We recommend thatthe Local Chief Executive:
a) require the Municipal Engineer to prepare the Inventory, Inspection
and Appraisal Form and Report of Waste Material and submit to the
Audit Team for validation in the case of properties costing below
P100,000.00 and referral to COA Regional Director for inspection on
items costing more than P100,000.00,
b) require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to
recompute the cost of the damaged properties based on the prescribed
formula provided by the DBM as contained in the Manual on Disposal
of Government Property; and
c) require the Municipal Accountant to record the loss based on the
approved Request for Relief from Property Accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive submit an explanation why
the 21 spaces in the B-Side Commercial were awarded to operators without
contract.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a. submit to the Audit Team for evaluation a copy of all the Lease
Contracts of the B-Side Commercial and all the other business spaces
of the LGU.
b. submit to the Municipal Accountant, a list of uncollected rent of all
lessees of business spaces of the LGU including the market stalls as of
December 31, 2021, for proper recognition in the books of accounts.

We further recommended that the Municipal Legal Officer review the existing
contracts and revise these to include the latest approved rates.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize in the
books the uncollected rent as of December 31, 2021 to completely account the
rents earned for CY 2021 and achieve a fair presentation of the accounts in the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant and all other officials concerned to
submit at the soonest possible time the lacking DVs and justification
why these were not submitted on time,
b. henceforth, enjoin the personnel concerned to consistently observe the
submission of accounts and financial reports within the reglementary
period pursuant to Section 107 of PD No. 1445 and pertinent provisions
of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995; and
c. Take appropriate actions against officials/employees concerned for the
repeated failure to adhere to the provisions of the afore-cited laws,
rules and regulations.

We recommended that the PESO Manager and Sug-Ong Farmers Association:
(i) render satisfactory explanation on the inoperative DOLE Livelihood
Program; and
(ii) coordinate with DOLE Region Office for proper and necessary actions
to be taken for the project.

We recommended that the following measures for future projects of similar
nature be undertaken:
a. Proper consultation and conduct of feasibility studies to be taken
before project implementation, where the specific needs of the targeted
beneficiaries are categorically identified. This way, the projects shall
be more responsive to the felt need of the beneficiaries to avoid wastage
of program funds;
b. To implement the project, a Memorandum of Agreement has to be
drafted between the Municipality of Hilongos and Beneficiary (Sug-
ong Farmer’s Association) where the duties and responsibilities of the
beneficiaries are properly outlined. This however, should reconcile
with the provisions of the prior MOA between the Municipality of
Hilongos with the Source Agency (DOLE); and
c. Coordinate with the Source Agency (DOLE) for the turn-over of the
tools and equipment to the beneficiaries of the projects. This measure
shall not only relieve the municipal government of the accountabilities
but shall also give responsibility to the party who maintains the actual
possession over the project.

We recommended that:
5. the Municipal Budget Officer include in the 20% DF only projects which are capital in nature;
6. the Contractor refund the equivalent to the amount of the unworked items; and
7. the Municipal Engineer to conduct actual inspection of projects before signing the Statement of Work Accomplishment; and
8. the Municipal Mayor create an Inspectorate Team who will conduct the inspection of completed projects prior to final payment.
9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Engineer implement all development projects in the AIP to ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% DF.
observations.managementAction: The Contractor responsible has been informed on this matter. An Inspectorate Team has been created through an Executive Order and inspection is conducted by the Municipal Engineer before signing the SWA. Another audit observation on the Implementation of PPAs is discussed in Part II No. 8 of this report.

The HR and Organizational Structure was assessed and recommendation on appropriate solutions are being processed. The HRM Unit drafted revised policies and guidelines for approval. Job Orders have been limited; additional staff were hired; selected Job Orders were placed under DOLE-SPES and PSA enumerators. Applicants are required to submit PDS, biodata, birth certificate and marriage certificate as primary IDs. Orientation on rules and regulations to Job Orders is conducted. The LGU placed additional 3 Job Orders May 04, 2020. The HRMO will design performance metrics and require submission of quantified accomplishment reports to support payroll. Discussion and referral with Municipal Legal Officer and Department Heads is ongoing regarding prohibited hirings. The RTWPB mandate is being followed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Solid Waste Management Board:
47. Intensify the campaign on waste segregation; 
48. Assign appropriate number of JOs to operate the equipment; and
49. Monitor the output of each equipment.
observations.managementAction: Intensify the campaign on waste segregation. Hired additional 3 Job Orders last May 04, 2020. Maintained monitoring logbook for the output of each equipment. Implementation is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor constitute an Internal Audit Service Unit in the organizational structure of the Municipality of Hilongos and assign personnel therein to perform the functions required by the said office.
observations.managementAction: An Internal Audit Specialist (IAS) has been designated. However, no one was assigned in 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should create:
a. an Inventory Committee to conduct a physical inventory of PPE owned by
the Municipality following the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of
physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE Items found at station, and
disposition for non-existing/missing PPE Items, for the one-time cleansing
of PPE account balances of the LGU as prescribed under COA Circular No.
2020-006.
b. a Disposal Committee to take charge with the disposal of unserviceable
properties following the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 89-
296.

We also recommended that the Accounting Office and Property Unit undertake
collaborative procedures on keeping of records under Items 6.3.1 and 6.3.2 and
disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs without available record
of accountability under Item 7of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to ensure that all
PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and
that the Property Cards maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs
maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled. If applicable, initiate the
derecognition procedures under Item 8 of the same Circular.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipality should comply with the
requirement of marking its vehicle “For Official Use Only” and using red plates.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should:
a. create an Inventory Committee that shall conduct physical count of all
inventories at year end;
b. require the Inventory Committee, the property officer, and the Municipal
Accountant to coordinate and reconcile the inventory balance in the RPCI
with the property and accounting records;
c. submits a copy of the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than January 31,
of the ensuing year.
d. expands the responsibilities of the incumbent property officer to include
the maintenance of complete property records, be it for inventory or for
PPEs, and the reconciliation of property records with the accounting
records and the RPCI.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman should submit justification why the
posting requirement was not complied with and the required documents were
not attached to the voucher.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should submit justification why the
voucher was processed and endorsed for payment despite the lack of the
required documents.

We also recommended that the person in-charge in the distribution of welfare
goods should submit the distribution lists for the vegetables and the 182 kilos
of dressed chicken distributed.

Henceforth, management should comply with pertinent requirements before
payment is made to avoid audit suspensions.

We recommended that the SRA paid be refunded by the individual recipients
without prejudice to the joint and solidary liability of those who authorized and
certified the legality and propriety of the payment.

We also recommended that, henceforth, the concerned municipal officials
should only approve transactions which are duly authorized by law to avoid
disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer should submit, for the Audit
Team’s evaluation, justification on the difference between the quantities of
materials per detailed estimates as against the materials actually used and
installed in the two projects, without prejudice to the issuance of notice of
disallowance for ₱56,630.00 deficiency found in audit.

Also, we recommended that the Inspectorate Team should exercise diligence in
the inspection of completed projects to make sure that all materials and
procedures prescribed conformed with the Program of Work or Detailed
Estimates.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should:
a. issue an order assigning to specific person the responsibility of managing
the Municipality’s warehouse for relief goods, including preserving the
these goods from further deterioration;
b. create and/or activate the Inventory Committee that will count and
segregate spoiled and unspoiled goods; and
cause the immediate distribution of unspoiled goods and
disposal/destruction of spoiled items to give space to newer stocks, if any,
subject to proper documentation and recording procedures prescribed
under existing regulations;

We also recommended that management should look for other means of
responding to food emergency other than stock piling if proper handling of
stocks could not be made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should see to it that the 20% DF
be optimally utilized for priority programs/ projects and activities for the
benefit of the constituents and the local government itself in accordance with
the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1, and

We also recommended that members of the Municipal Development Council
should review the AIP of the LGU to identify which project should be
considered topmost priority that can be implemented during the year and re-
appropriate the funds for those which are not urgent and not viable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instructs the:
a. members of the Municipal Development Council to conduct extensive
planning in the preparation of Program of Works (POWs) of succeeding
projects to avoid similar problems that cause additional works and/ or
several variation orders during the project implementation; and
b. Municipal Engineer to:
1) submit an explanation in certifying the correctness of the SWA despite
the noted defects and deficiencies in materials and non-deduction
thereof from the billings of the contract;
2) direct the Resident/ Project Engineer to enforce strict supervision on
project implementation, conduct thorough inspection on validation of
the actual accomplishment on all infrastructure projects submitted for
billing and prepare status reports showing the accurate work
accomplished as compared with the Contractor’s SWA; and
3) ensure the compliance of the contractor with the approved program of
works;
4) require the contractor to post signage/ billboard at the start and end of
the project site and should see to it that it is in accordance with the
standard form required under COA Circular No. 2013-004.
5) inform the Audit Team, through the Municipal Mayor, that the
billboard had been installed within ten days after award of the project
as required under Section 3.1 of the same circular.
6) prepare and submit the lacking documents mentioned under
paragraph no. 1.17 and as required under Section 9.1.1 and 9.1.1.4 of
COA Circular 2012-001.

Moving forward, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that payments to
contractors be made based on complete documents pursuant to Section 4(6) of
PD 1445 and guidelines under COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), BAC
Secretariat and/or Municipal Engineering Office should furnish the Audit Team
with copies of approved contracts and all the documents forming part thereof
within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No.
2009-001, dated February 12, 2009 to give the Audit Team enough time to review
the same, in post-audit.

We also recommended that the Inspection and Acceptance Committee should
prepare Inspection and Acceptance Report (IAR) and send notice of every
delivery to the Audit Team within 24 hours after the acceptance of delivered
items has been made, either partial or complete.

We recommended that the management should submit to the Audit Team the
Delivery Receipts and the corresponding IAR for the above deliveries, and
discontinue the practice of accepting food deliveries without specific IARs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit for the
Audit Team’s evaluation a justification why payment was made on the DVs even
without the Delivery Receipt and the corresponding IAR. She should conduct
a briefing of all Inspectors on how to conduct inspection of deliveries and to
prepare an Inspection Report.

Moving forward, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that payments to
suppliers/ creditors be made based on complete documents to support the claims
pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and guidelines under COA Circular 2012-
001.

1. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit the certified list of delinquent market stallholders to the Municipal Accountant such that the uncollected public market rentals every year- end, so that the Municipal Accountant can correctly recognize the same in the books of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:

20. issue a directive to the Punong Barangays who are recipients of the materials to submit a report on its utilization including proof of the completed project; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
30. the Members of the Oversight Committee: (1) review the appropriate rates and charges imposed by the LGU; (2) compute the appropriate rates for each services rendered by the LGU, and; (3) submit to the Mayor the proposed the new revisions of the rates and charges. 
31. the Municipal Mayor, as Chairperson of the Oversight Committee, immediately recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan the new tariffs.

33. the Municipal Treasurer collect the correct amount of lease rentals strictly in accordance with the current Municipal Revenue Code, without using discretion or preference. Follow any adjusted rates once the revision of the LGU’s Revenue Code is approved.

The Oversight Committee and the reconstituted Oversight Committee took consideration of the revised rates and charges. The Office of the Mayor will forward an urgent measure requested the Sanggunian Bayan to fast track the deliberation on the revised Revenue Code. The MTO will implement the recommended revised rates once approved. The Municipal Vice Mayor to facilitate issuance of local revenue ordinance. Municipal Treasurer to collect the correct amount of lease rentals. Implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
34. Issue Notices to Vacate to all delinquent stallholders with unpaid rental obligations for delinquent stallholders.

36. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer collect rentals every month from market stallholders and conduct random inspection of business premises to confirm the existence of Business Permits as required in Municipal Revenue Code of 2006.

The MTO is set to discuss with the Mayor on the matter of Mayor’s Closure Order to business owners without business permit. observations.recommendation:

37. Issue Notices to Vacate to all stallholders with unpaid rental obligations for three or more months pursuant to paragraph 1 of the Contract to Lease.
38. Issue Closure Order to business owners to vacate those occupying multiple stalls. 
We also recommended that the collectors from the Municipal Treasurer collect rentals every month from market stallholders and conduct random inspection of business premises to confirm the existence of Business Permits as required in Municipal Revenue Code of 2006.
observations.managementAction: The MTO issued delinquent notices to vacate to those delinquent stallholders. The current Municipal Treasurer started to effect appropriate closure of stalls owned by multiple stall occupants. All stall holders in the New Commercial Center vacated the units in question. The LGU is in the process of executing the Contract of Lease with Metro Retail Stores Group, Inc.

observations.recommendation:

The Municipal Treasurer is in the process of executing a Contract of Lease with Metro Retail Stores Group, Inc.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
44. discontinue the approval of excessive expenditures and those that are not responsive to the austerity measures directed in COA Circular No. 2012-003; and
45. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the documentary requirements of the cash advances made against the municipality’s fund, and henceforth, stop the processing of cash advances which are not supported with adequate documents.
observations.managementAction: New guidelines have been established on the propriety of the sports activities vis-à-vis prices. The LGU discontinued the excessive expenditures. observations.recommendation: We recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant withhold the applicable withholding tax rate from the honoraria given to Resource Speakers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
50. the Municipal Mayor incorporate in their GAD Plan the establishment of Lactation Station and ensure the accomplishment of the same; and
51. We also recommended that the Municipal DSWDO develop the program to help survivors of psychological and mental health as provided for in the municipality’s GAD Code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Revenue Collectors remit the collections daily and intact in the bank and cease the practice of retaining part of their collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor establish CPES Unit who will be responsible in the evaluation of prospective implementing entities using CPES implementing evaluation for our Performance evaluation in the qualification/eligibility screening of implementing contractors.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has maintained and established CPES guidelines and CPES unit who will evaluate the contractors. Implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare an updated list of all real property tax delinquencies and submit the same to the Sangguniang Bayan before December 31st of the year succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection either by administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer will henceforth prepare an updated list of delinquents. After the list of delinquents are updated such will be submitted to the Sanggunian Bayan before December 31st.

observations.recommendation: Establish a CPES Implementing Unit who will be responsible in the evaluation of prospective bidders using the Constructors Evaluation Performance Summary and the implementating of the CPES guidelines in the pre-qualification of bidders, awarding of contracts and monitoring performance in the prosecution of government projects.
observations.managementAction: The Unit was not established by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies and submit the same to the Sangguniang Bayan concerned.
observations.managementAction: Certified list of all real property tax delinquencies was not provided to the offices concerned.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor and its department officials to devise procedures to maximize and sustain the operations of the Commercial Center and assign a focal person who will manage its operations and the necessary utilities such as water and Terminal for local transportation. Also, prohibit the construction of makeshift structures within the vicinity and encourage the use of the stalls at the center instead.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor and its department officials devised procedures to maximize and sustain the operations of the Commercial Center and assigned a focal person. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:
1. All Department Heads to:
a) prepare Requisition Issue Slips every time an item is issued from its
stock and submit the Monthly Report of Supplies and Materials
Issued (MRSMI) duly supported with the RIS to the Accounting
Office for recording in the books;
b) maintain Stock Card for each item and reconcile the same with the
Supplies Ledger Card in the Accounting Office;
c) conduct physical count of inventories and submit a copy of Report of
Physical Count of Inventories to the Office of the Accountant and the
Audit Team.
2. The Municipal Accountant to:
a) review the composition of the Other Supplies and Materials Inventory
account to identify the Department/employee to whom the Inventory
Custodian Slip (ICS) will be issued before the same is removed from the
books;
b) prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the issuances of the
inventories based on the submitted MRSMI.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a.) Develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and
maximize collection of Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivables.
b) Deputize the Barangay Treasurer, in addition to the 5 collectors of the
Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay,
provided that the Barangay Treasurer is properly bonded for the purpose.
c) Issue a notice of delinquency to each concerned property owner and post
the same at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in a publicly
accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay of the Local
Government Unit of Hilongos.
d) Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which
remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year, and a statement of
the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment, and submit
the same to the Sanggunian concerned on or before December thirty-first
(31st) of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the
delinquencies were incurred, with a request for assistance in the enforcement
of the remedies for collection either by administrative action thru levy on real
property or by judicial action.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Heads of
departments who are in-charge with the implementation of projects funded by NGAs
to ensure that the projects are implemented and completed within the period
prescribed by the source agencies so as to achieve the intended purpose of the funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure the optimal utilization
of the 20% DF through the full implementation of development projects included in
the AIP.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit his collections to the
LGU depository bank intact and daily or not later than the next banking day to avoid
possible loss of LGU funds.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to prepare and submit a Daily Cash Position Report to monitor the funds
of the LGU and ensure that deposits are made by the Treasurer to fund checks signed
by them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Require the designated Chairman of the Physical Inventory Committee
to prepare and submit an inventory report of all insurable properties of
the Municipality and submit the list to the General Insurance Fund of the
GSIS for processing of the insurance coverage of all insurable
properties to ensure that the LGU will be indemnified in case of
loss, destruction and or damages.
2. Secure the Insurance Contract from LIBI to carefully examine its terms
and conditions and determine the agency’s rights and protect its
interest.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and its department officials
devise procedures to maximize and sustain the operations of the Commercial Center
and assign a focal person who will manage its operations and the necessary utilities
such as water and a Terminal for local transportation. Also, prohibit the construction
of makeshift structures within the vicinity and encourage the use of the stalls at the center
instead.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board
approve the use of the Special Education Fund only if such expenses are for the
improvement of school facilities and its operations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the MDRRMO:
1. Include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan the emergency equipment,
supplies and medicines necessary to be loaded and stacked in
the ambulance vehicles, and purchase the same immediately;
2. Prohibit the use of the Rescue vehicles as utility service vehicles of LGU
Employees, and;
3. Refill the Fire Extinguishers with fresh chemicals to ensure its readiness in
case of fire.

We recommended that the Local Solid Waste Management Board:
1. Monitor closely the implementation of the Municipal Solid Waste
Management Plan such as the improvement of the operation of the
Materials Recovery Facility by providing adequate water supply to clean
the plastic garbage;
2. Make the bioreactors operational as soon as possible, otherwise, consider
another composting method to reduce its solid waste to an acceptable level.
3. Expand the Residual Containment Area to accommodate fully the LGUs
residual wastes.

observations.recommendation:
We  recommend   that  the   Local   Chief   Executive    require   the
Committee on Physical  Inventory to complete  the  annual   physical   count
of  LGU assets  and  submit  the  required  Report  on  Physical   Count  of
Property , Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE)  not later than January  31 of  each
 year.


      Further, require the designated Property Officer to maintain  property
cards  for  monitoring  and  reconciliation  purposes  with  the  accounting
records.

We recommend  the  Municipal  Accountant  record  in the   books   of
accounts  of the LGU  the equipment   received   from  the   Department   of
Agriculture, Regional Office No. VIII to reflect the accurate asset  account
  in  the   financial   statements.   Subsidiary  ledgers  should  also   be
maintained.


      Further, require the Municipal Agriculturist to inform the  Municipal
Accountant and furnish documents pertaining thereto, whenever  equipment  or
other items are received by him from other agencies  for recording the  same
in the LGU’s books of accounts and for monitoring purposes.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive  designate  the  Municipal
Assessor to take charge with the titling of the real properties in the  name
of the municipality and designate the Municipal Treasurer  to  take  custody
of the documents related thereto.


      We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant trace the  acquisition
cost or appraised value of the recorded Land account to support  the  amount
recorded  in  the  books.   Property  cards  and  ledger  cards  should   be
maintained for these properties.

We recommend that the Agency Head exhaust all remedies to collect  the
receivables which are feasible for collection.


      We also recommend  that the  Local  Chief  Executive  file the request
for authority to write off  the  dormant  receivables  whose  collectability
could not be ascertained as provided for in Section  8.2  and  8.3   of  COA
Circular No. 2016-005  dated December  19, 2016 for a fair  presentation  of
these accounts in  the financial statements.

We  recommend  that  the  Municipal   Mayor   direct   the   Municipal
Agriculturist  and  the  Municipal    Engineer      to   speed-up     the
implementation  and completion  of  the   programs   and  projects   funded
by  the  National  Government  in order   to provide  the constituents  of
the   Municipality  the  benefits  that  could   be   derived   therefrom
particularly  the   livelihood project.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor submit a justification  as  to  why
the 20% development fund was utilized to procure CCTV  cameras  and  AVRs
which were not in accordance with the purpose for which  the  funds  were
intended.


      Also, the Municipal Planning Development  Officer  (MPDO)  include  in
the LGUs Annual Investment  Plan  priority projects  which  are  capital  in
nature and those authorized to be funded from the 20%  Development  Fund  in
order  to  attain  environmental  and  socio-economic  development  in   the
Municipality of Hilongos.


      Lastly,  we  recommend  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  require  the
submission of complete and necessary documents  for  each  disbursements  to
validate the transactions and avoid audit suspensions.

We recommend that the Municipal DRRMO maximize   the  utilization   of
the MDRRMF  in the Trust  Fund by including  in the MDRRMF  Investment  Plan
programs, projects  and activities  which will ensure readiness of the   LGU
in times of calamity.

We recommend that the Local School Board include in the SEF budget and
utilize the same for  programs/projects/activities  specifically  authorized
in Section 272 of RA 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint  Circular,  with  emphasis
on improvement of school facilities, in order to maximize  the use of the
SEF for its intended purpose.

We recommend that the BAC strictly evaluate thoroughly the eligibility
documents of all bidders so that only qualified  bidders  are  awarded  with
contracts and disallowances in post-audit will be avoided.


      We  also  recommend  that   the   BAC   Secretariat   supplement   its
advertisements of invitation to bid by adopting  the  policy  under  Section
363 of R.A.   7160  prescribing  the  sending  of  invitations  by  mail  to
prospective bidders in the locality or nearby  areas especially in cases  of
 high-value procurement.    At  all  times,   the  BAC  should  ensure  wide
dissemination of   information about the procurement activities of  the  LGU
so that qualified  and eligible bidders can  participate   and  transparency
and  competitiveness in all its procurement  processes  will be ensured.

This is a reiterated audit observation thus we recommend that the Municipal Treasurer be directed to stop immediately the practice of holding large amount of undeposited collections and to deposit collections promptly and intact.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the Mun. Treasurer to deposit collections promptly and intact.
observations.reason: There were instances where deposits were delayed thus collections were retained in the hands of the Mun. Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We  recommend  for  the   regular conduct of physical inventory taking on all properties to validate all recorded balances for the inventory accounts as well as PPE items.  To facilitate said inventory count: Each department may conduct physical inventory on all properties situated within its office, to be submitted for consolidation with those prepared by other offices. In the absence of GSO, designate property custodian in the treasurer’s office to take custody over all properties and who will be responsible for the maintenance of PPE cards. Create a committee who will conduct physical inventory taking regularly for all inventories as well as PPE items. The inventory report shall be made and will be submitted to the resident auditor assigned in the local government unit not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: The LCE issued an Office Memorandum Order for the creation of Disposal and Appraisal Committee, designating a Property Custodian from Treasurer Office. Office Memorandum No. 2016-03 has issued to all Department Heads to Conduct Physical Inventory Count of all PPE’s in their respective Department and submit the same on or before December 31, every year and year thereafter.
observations.reason: Lack of personnel to focus on the inventory of PPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management intensify its tax collection drive by issuing regularly billings/notice to taxpayers and making personal follow-ups constantly on delinquent ones.
